question: A fintech team uses CodePipeline to deliver a settlement service. After unit tests in CodeBuild succeed, the build artifact is deployed to a staging ECS cluster, then to production via blue/green. Auditors require that any pipeline execution that reaches production must be traceable to a signed Git tag and an approved change ticket ID, with both values stored immutably for seven years. Which approach satisfies the requirement with the least custom code?
options: A. Configure the source stage to trigger only on tags matching v*, add a manual approval action that captures the ticket ID in its comment, and rely on CodePipeline execution history.

B. Use a CodeBuild pre-build step to verify the signed tag with git verify-tag, write tag and ticket ID into an S3 Object Lock compliance-mode bucket via a Lambda action, and reference the object key in pipeline variables.
C. Store ticket IDs in DynamoDB with TTL of seven years and validate signatures inside an inline Lambda invoke action.
D. Use CodeArtifact domains with retention policies to keep the build outputs and tag metadata.
explanation: S3 Object Lock in compliance mode prevents deletion or overwrite for the configured retention period, which is what auditors mean by immutable. Verifying the signature in CodeBuild and recording both values to a locked bucket gives a tamper-evident trail. Pipeline execution history (A) is not retained for seven years and approval comments are mutable until execution ends. DynamoDB (C) has no native immutability and TTL deletes records. CodeArtifact (D) stores packages, not arbitrary audit metadata.

question: A SaaS company uses CodeDeploy with the Lambda compute platform to ship a public API. They want 10% of traffic on the new version for 15 minutes, then automatic promotion to 100% only if no CloudWatch alarm fires and a synthetic Canary run succeeds. The team also needs a single rollback signal that captures both pre-traffic and post-traffic validation. Which configuration meets the requirement?
options: A. CodeDeployDefault.LambdaCanary10Percent15Minutes deployment configuration, alarms attached to the deployment group, and BeforeAllowTraffic plus AfterAllowTraffic hooks invoking the Synthetics canary via Lambda.
B. CodeDeployDefault.LambdaLinear10PercentEvery1Minute with EventBridge rules invoking the canary on each shift.
C. CodeDeployDefault.LambdaAllAtOnce with a CloudWatch composite alarm gated on the canary metric.
D. A custom traffic shifting configuration in API Gateway stage variables, polled by a Step Functions workflow.
explanation: LambdaCanary10Percent15Minutes shifts 10% immediately and the rest after 15 minutes, matching the requirement. Attaching CloudWatch alarms to the deployment group triggers automatic rollback, and BeforeAllowTraffic/AfterAllowTraffic lifecycle hooks are exactly the pre- and post-traffic validation points. Linear (B) shifts continuously and does not match a 10/100 pattern. AllAtOnce (C) provides no canary phase. Stage variables (D) abandon the managed CodeDeploy rollback signal entirely.

question: A gaming studio runs CodePipeline across three accounts: tools (pipeline), staging, and prod. Engineers report that the CloudFormation deploy action in the prod stage fails with AccessDenied when assuming the cross-account role, but only on stacks that include KMS-encrypted SSM parameters. Staging deployments work. What is the most likely cause?
options: A. The pipeline service role in tools is missing kms:GenerateDataKey on the prod KMS key.
B. The CloudFormation execution role in prod can decrypt the parameters, but the prod KMS key policy does not allow the cross-account artifact bucket key, so artifacts cannot be read.
C. The CMK used to encrypt the artifact bucket in tools does not include the prod CloudFormation role and the prod CodePipeline cross-account role as key users.
D. CodePipeline does not support cross-account deploys for stacks that reference SecureString parameters.
explanation: Cross-account pipelines require the artifact-bucket KMS key in the tools account to grant kms:Decrypt to every role in the target account that touches the artifact (the cross-account role and the CloudFormation execution role). When a stack template references a SecureString, CloudFormation reads it at deploy time, but the failure here is at artifact retrieval, before parameter resolution. (A) is the wrong key. (B) confuses key directions. (D) is false.

question: A healthcare ISV builds container images in CodeBuild and pushes to ECR. Compliance requires a signed attestation that the image was built from a specific commit hash, that no high or critical CVEs were detected, and that the artifact was reviewed by a second engineer before the image tag becomes mutable in production. Which combination is the most maintainable?
options: A. Use ECR image scanning on push, IAM Access Analyzer for review, and pipeline manual approval.

B. Use CodeBuild to produce an in-toto attestation, push the image to ECR with immutable tags, run Inspector v2 enhanced scanning, gate promotion with a CodePipeline manual approval that records the reviewer ARN, and use a Lambda action to attach a Signer signature on success.
C. Switch to CodeArtifact for container images and rely on its built-in signing.
D. Run Trivy in CodeBuild, push to a public ECR Public repository with mutable tags, and rely on GitHub branch protection.
explanation: ECR supports tag immutability per repository, Inspector v2 provides continuous CVE scanning beyond the basic ECR scan, and AWS Signer (with Notation) attaches verifiable signatures. Recording the reviewer in the manual approval and producing an in-toto attestation in CodeBuild gives provenance back to the commit. (A) lacks signing and provenance. (C) CodeArtifact does not host container images. (D) public repos and mutable tags violate the requirement.

question: An e-commerce team uses GitHub Actions to build artifacts and CodePipeline to deploy them. They want GitHub Actions to push artifacts directly into the pipeline source stage without long-lived AWS keys. Which approach is correct?
options: A. Configure GitHub Actions OIDC with an IAM role that has s3:PutObject on the pipeline artifact bucket and use a custom source action.

B. Configure CodeStar Connections to GitHub, use the V2 GitHub source action, and let pipeline executions be triggered by branch pushes; build in GitHub Actions and publish artifacts to an S3 bucket configured as a separate S3 source action triggered via EventBridge with the OIDC role.
C. Store a GitHub personal access token in Secrets Manager and rotate weekly.
D. Use AWS CodeBuild webhooks against GitHub and skip CodePipeline entirely.
explanation: CodeStar Connections handle source events without static tokens, and GitHub Actions can assume a short-lived role through GitHub OIDC to upload to S3. EventBridge on S3 object create then triggers the pipeline source action that polls the bucket. (A) ignores the requirement to feed artifacts into the pipeline. (C) uses long-lived credentials, exactly what the requirement forbids. (D) drops the pipeline orchestration the team already uses.

question: A team needs CodeBuild jobs to install private npm packages from a CodeArtifact repository in another account. The CI account is dev-tools and the CodeArtifact domain is in shared-services. Which configuration grants least-privilege access?
options: A. Add a domain policy in shared-services granting codeartifact:GetAuthorizationToken and a repository policy granting codeartifact:ReadFromRepository to the CodeBuild service role principal in dev-tools, and have buildspec call aws codeartifact login.
B. Make the CodeArtifact repository public.
C. Set up a VPC endpoint for CodeArtifact and rely on endpoint policies alone.
D. Create an IAM user with long-lived keys in shared-services and inject the credentials via Secrets Manager.
explanation: CodeArtifact uses a domain policy plus repository policy for cross-account access. Grant GetAuthorizationToken on the domain and ReadFromRepository on the repository to the build role principal, then call codeartifact login to fetch a 12-hour token. (B) violates least privilege. (C) endpoints control network path, not authorization. (D) re-introduces long-lived keys.

question: A microservices team runs blue/green deploys on ECS with CodeDeploy. They observed that target group draining succeeds but the new task set occasionally serves errors during deployments. The new task definition pulls a config blob from S3 at startup. They want to halt the deploy automatically if the new task set serves more than 1% 5xx during the test traffic window. Which configuration is correct?
options: A. Add an AfterAllowTestTraffic lifecycle hook with a Lambda that calls GetMetricStatistics and returns Failed when the threshold is exceeded.
B. Use a CloudWatch alarm on the test target group HTTPCode_Target_5XX_Count and attach it to the deployment group's alarm list.
C. Reduce the blue task set's deregistration delay to zero.
D. Switch to in-place deploys and rely on ECS circuit breaker.
explanation: CodeDeploy ECS blue/green supports a separate test listener that routes to the green task set; a CloudWatch alarm on that test target group's 5XX count attached to the deployment group's alarm configuration triggers automatic rollback. (A) is workable but more code than needed and lacks the built-in alarm-based rollback. (C) addresses connection draining, not error rate. (D) loses blue/green entirely.

question: A platform team wants every pull request opened against the main branch in CodeCommit to automatically run a CodeBuild project that posts results back to the PR. Which event-driven design is appropriate?
options: A. EventBridge rule on CodeCommit pullRequestCreated and pullRequestSourceBranchUpdated events that targets a Lambda which calls StartBuild with sourceVersion set to the PR commit, then on build complete posts a comment via PostCommentForPullRequest.
B. Trigger CodeBuild via SNS on every push to main and have it diff against the PR.
C. Use CodePipeline with a CodeCommit source filtered on pullRequestCreated.
D. Set a Git pre-receive hook in CodeCommit to call CodeBuild.
explanation: EventBridge surfaces CodeCommit PR events, including the PR ID and source commit. A Lambda starts the build at the right commit and uses PostCommentForPullRequest to write back results. (B) is too coarse and can run on commits that are not PRs. (C) CodePipeline source actions only trigger on branch updates, not PR lifecycle events. (D) CodeCommit does not expose pre-receive hooks.

question: A team's CodePipeline succeeds in dev but fails the CloudFormation deploy in prod with InsufficientCapabilitiesException only when the template introduces a new IAM role. The pipeline has CAPABILITY_NAMED_IAM in dev. What is the correct remediation?
options: A. Switch the prod deploy action to a CreateChangeSet/ExecuteChangeSet pair using CAPABILITY_NAMED_IAM.
B. Add CAPABILITY_AUTO_EXPAND to the prod action.
C. Set the prod deploy action's Capabilities to CAPABILITY_NAMED_IAM and grant iam:CreateRole to the CloudFormation execution role.
D. Recreate the role manually and import it as an existing resource.
explanation: InsufficientCapabilitiesException specifically means the action is missing the IAM capability flag, so set CAPABILITY_NAMED_IAM (the role has a fixed name) and ensure the execution role can create it. (A) splits into change set but does not change the capability requirement. (B) AUTO_EXPAND is for transforms, not IAM. (D) manual import sidesteps automation.

question: A devops team automates database migrations as part of a CodePipeline. Migrations should run only once even if the deploy stage retries, and must be idempotent against concurrent pipeline executions across feature branches. Which design provides safe execution?
options: A. Run the migration in a CodeBuild action with a buildspec that uses a Liquibase changelog tracked in DATABASECHANGELOGLOCK on the target database.
B. Run migrations in a Lambda invoke action with no concurrency controls.
C. Disable retries on the deploy stage and assume engineers will not branch.
D. Use Step Functions Map state to run migrations in parallel across branches.
explanation: Liquibase (and Flyway) maintain a lock table that serializes concurrent runs and a changelog history that prevents re-applying the same change. Running this from CodeBuild gives logs and IAM, while the lock provides cross-execution safety. (B) creates the race the question asks to avoid. (C) is operationally fragile. (D) deliberately parallelizes mutations.

question: A fintech wants every CodeCommit commit to main to trigger a Lambda that validates the commit author is a member of a specific IAM Identity Center group. Which integration is correct?
options: A. Use a CodeCommit trigger that calls Lambda directly with the commit metadata.

B. EventBridge rule on the CodeCommit referenceUpdated event that targets a Lambda which calls GetCommit, then queries Identity Center via the IdentityStore API to check group membership; reject by force-pushing a revert if the check fails is unsafe so instead log to Security Hub.
C. Lambda subscribes to CloudTrail data events on CodeCommit and rewrites history if the author is not authorized.
D. Use IAM Access Analyzer findings to detect unauthorized commits.
explanation: EventBridge publishes referenceUpdated for branch changes; the Lambda has access to GetCommit and can map the IAM principal to an Identity Center user via IdentityStore. Rewriting history is destructive and out of scope, so logging a finding is the safe pattern. (A) CodeCommit triggers go to SNS or Lambda but lack the rich event filtering EventBridge provides. (C) data events do not enable history rewrites. (D) Access Analyzer checks resource policies.

question: A team deploys a Lambda function with CodeDeploy LambdaLinear10PercentEvery2Minutes. During production rollout, the Lambda's downstream RDS Proxy returns intermittent timeouts. The deployment did not roll back because no alarm was attached. Which combination provides the most useful guardrail?
options: A. CloudWatch alarm on the new alias's Errors metric (Statistic: Sum) and a composite alarm combining Errors with the function's Duration p99, attached to the deployment group.
B. CloudWatch alarm on TotalConcurrentExecutions across all functions.
C. Disable traffic shifting and deploy AllAtOnce.
D. Trigger rollback only via X-Ray service map analysis.
explanation: Lambda alias-level metrics include Errors and Duration scoped to the version receiving shifted traffic. A composite alarm catches both error spikes and latency regressions and rolls back automatically when wired to the deployment group. (B) is too broad. (C) removes the safety the question wants to keep. (D) X-Ray is not a deployment alarm source.

question: An organization uses CodePipeline V2 with execution modes. Two engineers merge to main minutes apart, and both pipeline executions enter the deploy stage simultaneously, racing to update the same CloudFormation stack. Which execution mode prevents this without serializing the entire pipeline?
options: A. PARALLEL execution mode with stage-level concurrency.
B. QUEUED execution mode, which queues new executions and processes them one at a time.

C. SUPERSEDED execution mode, which is the default and prevents simultaneous execution by replacing pending executions in earlier stages, while in-flight executions in later stages are not interrupted.
D. ROLLING execution mode.
explanation: QUEUED ensures executions run sequentially end-to-end so deploys cannot race. SUPERSEDED (the default) does not queue: a newer execution catches up in earlier stages and replaces pending older ones, but if the older one is already in deploy, both can land. PARALLEL allows independent executions and is opposite to what is wanted. ROLLING is not a CodePipeline mode.

question: A CodeBuild job needs a 50 GB working directory to assemble a machine-learning artifact, and the team wants persistent caching across runs without managing EFS quotas. Which configuration is best?
options: A. Use a custom AMI with a large EBS volume.
B. Use CodeBuild project cache type S3 with the relevant paths set in cache.paths, sized at the artifact root, accepting eventual consistency on layer reuse.
C. Use CodeBuild project cache type LOCAL with the SOURCE_CACHE, DOCKER_LAYER_CACHE, and CUSTOM_CACHE modes; combine with a larger compute type that provides ephemeral disk.
D. Mount EFS from the CodeBuild VPC and snapshot daily.
explanation: CodeBuild LOCAL cache reuses the build host between runs (best-effort) and supports multiple modes including DOCKER_LAYER_CACHE and CUSTOM_CACHE. Larger compute types provide more ephemeral disk and avoid quotas. S3 cache (B) is durable but adds upload/download time at 50 GB. (A) defeats managed builds. (D) reintroduces quota management.

question: A retail platform's CodePipeline includes a Lambda invoke action that calls a third-party API for compliance checks. Sometimes the API takes longer than 15 minutes, causing the action to time out. Which architectural change preserves the long-running call without violating CodePipeline limits?
options: A. Increase the Lambda timeout above 15 minutes.

B. Replace the Lambda action with a Step Functions state machine invoked via the AWS Step Functions action; have the state machine handle long polling and signal the action via PutJobSuccessResult or PutJobFailureResult through an attached Lambda.
C. Switch the action to a CodeBuild project with a 24-hour build timeout.
D. Run the check in parallel via SQS and ignore failures.
explanation: Step Functions has a 1-year execution limit, perfect for waiting on a slow API. CodePipeline custom actions and Step Functions actions can be completed asynchronously by calling PutJobSuccessResult or PutJobFailureResult. (A) Lambda max is 15 minutes. (C) buys time but loses native orchestration. (D) discards the compliance signal.

question: A team uses AWS SAM with sam pipeline init and CodePipeline. They want feature branches to deploy ephemeral environments that auto-delete after seven days. Which approach is appropriate?
options: A. Manually delete stacks weekly.
B. Use a CloudFormation StackSet with a delete operation.

C. Generate a unique stack name per branch, tag the stack with an Expiration tag of seven days hence, and run a scheduled EventBridge rule that invokes a Lambda which lists stacks by tag and calls DeleteStack on expired ones.
D. Use AWS Backup lifecycle to expire stacks.
explanation: Tag-based discovery plus a scheduled cleaner is the standard ephemeral-environment pattern: stacks are deterministic, the lifecycle is encoded in the tag, and EventBridge gives reliable scheduling. (A) is manual. (B) StackSets are for replicating one stack across accounts, not feature-branch lifecycles. (D) AWS Backup does not manage CloudFormation stacks.

question: A media company's deployment runs CodeDeploy in-place to EC2 with the CodeDeployDefault.OneAtATime configuration. They report that long-lived sessions on the appserver are dropped during deploys because the ApplicationStop hook kills the process before clients drain. Which lifecycle hook adjustment helps without abandoning in-place?
options: A. Use BeforeBlockTraffic to deregister the instance from the load balancer and wait for connection draining before ApplicationStop runs.
B. Move the kill -9 into AfterAllowTraffic.
C. Set the deployment configuration to CodeDeployDefault.AllAtOnce.
D. Skip ApplicationStop entirely.
explanation: BeforeBlockTraffic runs while the instance is still in the load balancer and is the place to drain or quiesce. CodeDeploy automatically blocks traffic between BeforeBlockTraffic and AfterBlockTraffic so connections drain before ApplicationStop. (B) is after the new code is live. (C) makes the problem worse. (D) leaves stale processes.

question: A team runs CodePipeline with a CloudFormation deploy action and wants any deploy that produces a stack rollback to halt the pipeline AND open a Jira ticket with the resource that failed. Which integration is most direct?
options: A. Configure CloudFormation stack events to publish to an SNS topic; subscribe a Lambda that filters for ROLLBACK events, parses the resource status reason, and calls the Jira API. The deploy action will mark itself failed and the pipeline will halt naturally.
B. Poll DescribeStackEvents from a CodeBuild action every 30 seconds.
C. Use CloudTrail Lake queries on a 5-minute schedule.
D. Configure rollback triggers but they cannot notify external systems.
explanation: CloudFormation supports a NotificationARNs property pointing to SNS, which receives every stack event including ROLLBACK_IN_PROGRESS. A Lambda subscriber can extract LogicalResourceId and ResourceStatusReason and post to Jira; the failing deploy action stops the pipeline on its own. (B) is fragile polling. (C) introduces minutes of lag. (D) is incorrect: rollback triggers can target alarms, but SNS notifications are simpler for ticketing.

question: A company manages 250 AWS accounts under Organizations. They want to deploy a baseline VPC, IAM roles, and Config recorder to every member account when it is created, with drift remediation. Which approach is the most appropriate?
options: A. Use AWS Control Tower with Account Factory for Terraform and customizations through CfCT (Customizations for Control Tower) referencing CloudFormation StackSets with service-managed permissions and SERVICE_MANAGED auto-deployment to the OU.
B. Use a single CloudFormation template applied by a daily Lambda iterating accounts.
C. Use AWS Config aggregator to push baselines.
D. Use OpsWorks Stacks per account.
explanation: Control Tower handles new-account provisioning; CfCT layers customizations as StackSets. Service-managed StackSets with auto-deployment to an OU automatically deploy to new accounts and detect drift. (B) reinvents StackSets. (C) Config aggregates findings, it does not provision. (D) OpsWorks is legacy and does not address account vending.

question: A platform engineer needs to detect when an engineer manually edits a security group that is managed by CloudFormation. They want notification within minutes and a record of which property drifted. Which approach is best?
options: A. Schedule cloudformation detect-stack-drift every five minutes via EventBridge and publish DescribeStackResourceDrifts results that show MODIFIED to SNS.

B. Use AWS Config rule cloudformation-stack-drift-detection-check, which evaluates drift continuously by listening to API calls and surfaces drifted resources to Config and EventBridge with the property differences.
C. Enable termination protection on every stack.
D. Use CloudTrail event history filtering on AuthorizeSecurityGroupIngress.
explanation: The managed Config rule cloudformation-stack-drift-detection-check runs drift detection on a schedule and reports per-resource drift to Config; from there EventBridge can notify on noncompliance. (A) works but is more code and lacks Config history. (C) prevents deletion, not edits. (D) tells you a call happened but not whether the resource diverges from the template.

question: A regulated team wants every CloudFormation deployment in production to pause for human review before execution if it would replace an RDS instance, regardless of the change author. Which design enforces this?
options: A. Always perform CreateChangeSet then require a manual approval action that uses a Lambda to inspect the change set; if any Replacement is True for AWS::RDS::DBInstance the approval is mandatory and the pipeline routes through a separate approver group.
B. Tag stacks as production and rely on engineers to remember.
C. Use stack policies to deny replacement.
D. Configure CloudFormation drift detection to block replacements.
explanation: Change sets surface replacement intent before execution, and Lambda can parse Changes[].ResourceChange.Replacement. The pipeline routes to the appropriate approval group based on the parsed result. (B) is not enforcement. (C) stack policies would actually block the replacement, but the requirement is a review, not a hard deny; also stack policies do not pause. (D) drift detection observes after the fact.

question: An infra team writes CDK in TypeScript. They want runtime validation that rejects synthesis if a developer creates an S3 bucket without server-side encryption or with public access enabled. Which approach is preferred?
options: A. Add a custom Aspect that visits constructs at synth time, plus cdk-nag with the AwsSolutions pack and suppression governance through a code review.
B. Use IAM SCPs only.
C. Use AWS Config rules at runtime.
D. Add a Service Catalog product with the right defaults and forbid CDK.
explanation: CDK Aspects walk the construct tree at synth and can fail the build, and cdk-nag supplies vetted rule packs (AwsSolutions, NIST, HIPAA). Suppressions require explicit code annotation, providing review traceability. (B) blocks at runtime, not synth. (C) runtime detection is too late for IaC quality gates. (D) abandons CDK entirely.

question: A team uses Systems Manager Patch Manager for a fleet of 4,000 EC2 instances. They need staged patching across 4 patch groups (canary 5%, early 15%, broad 50%, late 30%) within a weekly maintenance window, with automatic rollback if CloudWatch alarms fire on the application. Which configuration meets the requirement?
options: A. Tag instances by patch group, define one maintenance window with four sequential tasks each targeting a tag, set MaxConcurrency per task to limit blast radius, attach a CloudWatch alarm to the maintenance window task to halt remaining tasks; for rollback rely on AMI baking and Auto Scaling instance refresh in case of failure.
B. Use Patch Manager AWS-RunPatchBaseline against all instances with MaxErrors=10%.
C. Use OpsWorks deployments scheduled weekly.
D. Use State Manager with a single association.
explanation: Maintenance Windows let you group tasks, target by tag, set MaxConcurrency for staging, and bind a CloudWatch alarm that halts remaining tasks if it fires. SSM Patch Manager itself does not roll back patches, so AMI rebaking plus Auto Scaling refresh provides recovery. (B) skips staging. (C) OpsWorks is legacy and does not provide patch baselines. (D) State Manager is for desired-state, not patch waves.

question: A compliance officer requires that every parameter under /prod/* in Parameter Store is encrypted with a customer-managed KMS key, and that any plaintext parameter in that path is automatically converted. Which combination is correct?
options: A. AWS Config managed rule ssm-parameter-tier-required.

B. AWS Config custom rule that lists parameters and reports NON_COMPLIANT when Type is String, paired with an SSM Automation runbook (custom) that re-creates the parameter as SecureString with the CMK and is invoked by Config remediation.
C. Service Control Policy denying ssm:PutParameter.
D. Lambda triggered by CloudTrail PutParameter that sets KMS by editing the existing parameter via UpdateParameter.
explanation: There is no built-in Config rule for plaintext-in-Parameter-Store, so a custom rule fits; remediation must re-create as SecureString because you cannot change Type via UpdateParameter. (A) checks tiers, not encryption. (C) blocks all writes including legitimate ones. (D) UpdateParameter cannot change Type from String to SecureString.

question: A platform team manages a baseline AMI built monthly with EC2 Image Builder. They need automatic distribution to 30 accounts across 6 regions, signed with a specific Image Builder distribution configuration, and immediate revocation when CVEs are discovered. Which approach is correct?
options: A. Use Image Builder pipelines with a distribution configuration that targets multiple Regions and shares the AMI with the Organization or specific accounts; integrate Inspector v2 findings via EventBridge to trigger an Automation runbook that deprecates the AMI in all regions.
B. Copy AMIs manually across accounts.
C. Use AMI Backup vaults for distribution.
D. Use S3 Cross-Region Replication of the snapshot files.
explanation: Image Builder distribution configurations target multiple Regions and share with target accounts or AWS Organizations IDs. The AMI deprecation API marks an AMI as deprecated in a Region without deletion, perfect for revocation. (B) is manual. (C) AWS Backup does not redistribute AMIs. (D) snapshots are not bootable AMIs after raw copy.

question: A team uses CloudFormation StackSets with self-managed permissions. After the central account moves under Organizations and switches to service-managed permissions, deployments to existing target accounts fail with TrustedAccess errors. What is the most likely cause?
options: A. Service-managed permissions require AWS CloudFormation StackSets to be enabled as a trusted service in Organizations and the management account must enable trusted access.
B. The OU was deleted.
C. Service-managed permissions require IAM users in each target account.
D. The CMK does not allow Organizations.
explanation: Service-managed StackSets rely on Organizations trusted access; without enabling it via EnableAWSServiceAccess (or in the console), StackSets cannot assume the AWSCloudFormationStackSetsOrgAdminRole. (B) would produce different errors. (C) self-managed used roles, service-managed uses Organizations-provisioned roles automatically. (D) is unrelated.

question: A team needs to manage hundreds of EC2 instances across hybrid (on-prem and cloud) without bastion hosts, with full audit of every interactive session including keystrokes and mouse, and the ability to break-glass cut sessions. Which approach is best?
options: A. SSH with CloudTrail logging.

B. Systems Manager Session Manager with KMS-encrypted session logs to S3 and CloudWatch Logs, hybrid activations for on-prem nodes, and an IAM policy condition on ssm:StartSession that requires a justification tag; admins can call TerminateSession to cut.
C. AWS Client VPN with full-tunnel and per-user routes.
D. AWS Direct Connect with private subnets and SSH bastions.
explanation: Session Manager records full session output to S3 and CloudWatch Logs (encrypted via KMS), supports hybrid via SSM activations, allows IAM policy conditions for justification, and supports TerminateSession for break-glass. SSH (A) lacks per-keystroke audit. (C) and (D) are network-layer and do not record sessions.

question: An engineering team wants to refactor a monolithic CloudFormation stack into nested stacks without downtime, while preserving stateful resources (RDS, DynamoDB). Which approach is correct?
options: A. Delete and recreate the stack.

B. Use the CloudFormation resource import feature to import the existing stateful resources into the new nested-stack architecture, validating with change sets at every step and using stack policies to protect imported resources.
C. Convert to Terraform first.
D. Use AWS Backup to migrate.
explanation: CloudFormation resource import lets you bring resources under management of a new stack without re-creating them; combined with change sets you can verify there is no drift, and stack policies prevent accidental replacement. (A) destroys data. (C) is unnecessary toolchain change. (D) AWS Backup is not a CloudFormation refactor tool.

question: A regulated industry team wants any CloudFormation update to specific stacks to require explicit override if it would delete a stateful resource. Which is the most direct enforcement?
options: A. Add a stack policy that DENY's Update:Replace and Update:Delete on the resources of type AWS::RDS::DBInstance and AWS::DynamoDB::Table; the pipeline strips the policy temporarily only after a manual override action.
B. Use IAM policies on the user.
C. Configure stack termination protection.
D. Use a deletion policy of Snapshot.
explanation: Stack policies are exactly for in-stack update protection. They support resource-type and logical-ID glob patterns and allow temporary override via SetStackPolicy or by passing StackPolicyDuringUpdate at update time. (B) cannot scope per resource within a stack. (C) prevents stack delete, not updates. (D) Snapshot mitigates loss but does not prevent the action.

question: A team adopts AWS Systems Manager State Manager to enforce that the CloudWatch agent is installed and running with a specific config on all production EC2 instances. They want guaranteed re-application within 30 minutes of drift. Which approach is correct?
options: A. State Manager association targeting the relevant tag with the AWS-ConfigureAWSPackage and AmazonCloudWatch-ManageAgent documents, with a schedule expression of rate(30 minutes) and ApplyOnlyAtCronInterval=false.
B. Run Command on demand.
C. SSM Maintenance Window scheduled monthly.
D. Create a Lambda that SSH's into instances every 30 minutes.
explanation: State Manager associations enforce desired state on a schedule and re-run when drift is detected. The combination of ConfigureAWSPackage and ManageAgent installs and configures the CloudWatch agent. (B) is one-shot. (C) monthly is too slow. (D) violates managed practices.

question: A team uses CloudFormation with the AWS::CloudFormation::Macro and AWS::Include transforms. After enabling SCPs that deny lambda:InvokeFunction except for specific tagged functions, deploys fail. What is happening?
options: A. Macros are processed by CloudFormation calling a Lambda; the SCP denies that invocation. Tag the macro Lambda appropriately or scope the SCP to exclude transforms.
B. AWS::Include is not supported.
C. Macros require lambda:CreateFunction.
D. AUTO_EXPAND must be removed.
explanation: Transforms execute by CloudFormation invoking the macro's backing Lambda; an SCP that filters InvokeFunction by tag will block them. The fix is to apply the expected tag to the macro Lambda or scope the SCP. (B) is incorrect. (C) is wrong: macros invoke, not create. (D) AUTO_EXPAND is required for nested transforms; removing breaks the build.

question: A platform offers self-service provisioning to developers. Requirements: developers cannot edit underlying CloudFormation, products have versioned templates, and access is by Identity Center group. Which AWS service fits?
options: A. AWS Service Catalog with portfolios shared via Organizations, products versioned, launch constraints providing the IAM role used by CloudFormation, and access granted to Identity Center groups.
B. Proton only.
C. CloudFormation Public Registry.
D. CodeCatalyst Projects.
explanation: Service Catalog is purpose-built for this: portfolios, product versions, launch constraints (so users do not need direct CFN permissions), and Identity Center group access. Proton is more code-oriented. The Public Registry is for resource types. CodeCatalyst is dev environment + CI, not provisioning catalogs.

question: An e-commerce site needs zero-RPO database failover across two Regions for an Aurora PostgreSQL cluster, with automatic failover triggered by Region-level alarms and a published DNS endpoint that does not change after failover. Which architecture meets the requirement?
options: A. Aurora Global Database with the secondary Region as a read replica; use Route 53 ARC routing controls in conjunction with a managed DNS endpoint and orchestrate failover via a Lambda invoked on a Region health alarm. Note RPO is typically under one second but not strictly zero; for RPO=0 use Aurora write forwarding or accept synchronous limitations.
B. RDS Multi-AZ across Regions.
C. DynamoDB Global Tables.
D. S3 Cross-Region Replication.
explanation: Aurora Global Database provides cross-Region replication with sub-second RPO (not strictly zero, but the closest available); Route 53 ARC routing controls let you flip a stable hosted-zone endpoint without DNS races. (B) RDS Multi-AZ is single-Region only. (C) different engine and constraints. (D) is for object storage, not transactional DB.

question: A SaaS product requires that during an AZ outage, the Auto Scaling group never launches an instance with stale boot configuration. The team uses launch templates and Auto Scaling lifecycle hooks. Which lifecycle hook strategy satisfies the requirement?
options: A. Use a launch lifecycle hook that holds the instance in Pending:Wait while a Lambda validates the boot configuration via SSM Run Command and either CompleteLifecycleAction Continue or Abandon.
B. Use a terminate lifecycle hook only.
C. Disable lifecycle hooks during an outage.
D. Use spot instances with capacity rebalancing.
explanation: Launch hooks pause new instances at Pending:Wait until validation completes, preventing them from joining the load balancer with stale config. (B) only protects on shutdown. (C) removes the safety net. (D) addresses interruption, not validation.

question: A healthcare backend uses an SQS standard queue feeding a Lambda. To meet the team's resilience SLO, every poison message must be retained for forensic analysis for 14 days, and processing must continue. Which configuration is correct?
options: A. Configure a dead-letter queue with maxReceiveCount=5 and MessageRetentionPeriod=1209600 seconds; on the Lambda also configure on-failure destinations to a separate forensic queue for the asynchronous invocations.
B. Use a FIFO queue and stop processing on failure.
C. Configure VisibilityTimeout=1209600.
D. Use Lambda concurrency 0 to drain manually.
explanation: SQS DLQs catch the message after the configured maxReceiveCount; the DLQ retention can be 14 days. Lambda async destinations capture the invocation record, complementing message-level retention. (B) blocks processing. (C) just delays redelivery. (D) blocks processing.

question: A trading platform requires the ability to fail an entire Region within 60 seconds with a single, manually-triggered control, and to prove this is testable in a game day. Which AWS service provides this control plane with high reliability?
options: A. Route 53 Application Recovery Controller (ARC) with routing controls hosted on a 5-Region data plane and safety rules to prevent dual-region active when invariants would be violated.
B. CloudWatch alarms with composite logic.
C. CloudFront origin failover only.
D. AWS Resilience Hub assessments.
explanation: Route 53 ARC routing controls run on a 5-Region cell-based data plane explicitly engineered to be available even during a Region outage; safety rules block invalid state combinations. (B) is monitoring, not control. (C) origin failover handles HTTP origins, not Region-level orchestration. (D) is design-time assessment.

question: An ALB-backed ECS service must survive a single-AZ outage with no observable customer impact. The team currently runs three tasks across two AZs. What is the most defensible configuration change?
options: A. Run with placement strategy spread on attribute:ecs.availability-zone across at least three AZs with a minimum healthy task count that survives loss of any one AZ; verify ALB cross-zone load balancing is enabled (default for ALB) and Auto Scaling capacity providers maintain capacity.
B. Use Fargate Spot exclusively.
C. Pin tasks to a single AZ to simplify networking.
D. Disable connection draining.
explanation: Spread across three AZs ensures that loss of one AZ leaves quorum, and ALB cross-zone load balancing prevents traffic concentration on the surviving AZs. (B) Spot can be reclaimed and is not an availability strategy alone. (C) is anti-pattern. (D) hurts behavior during scale events.

question: A serverless team uses API Gateway in front of Lambda. During a downstream outage they want the API to return cached responses instead of failing. Which is the most cost-effective mechanism?
options: A. Enable API Gateway stage caching with a TTL appropriate to the data; use cache key parameters and authorize cache invalidation; for finer control, use CloudFront with custom error caching in front.
B. Use ElastiCache Memcached only inside the Lambda.
C. Increase Lambda timeout.
D. Use Step Functions express workflows.
explanation: API Gateway stage cache returns cached responses while origins are unreachable until TTL expires; CloudFront in front extends cache resilience and supports custom error caching. (B) cache miss still hits the failed dependency. (C) does not turn errors into cache hits. (D) is unrelated.

question: A multi-account workload uses RDS Proxy in front of a primary RDS PostgreSQL. The team observed that during a Multi-AZ failover, application connections receive lingering errors for 90 seconds. They need failover transparency under 30 seconds. Which change helps the most?
options: A. Use RDS Proxy with the ConnectionBorrowTimeout tuned, IAM authentication, and ensure the application uses short transactions; RDS Proxy holds the database connection through failover and is the supported way to reduce client impact.
B. Switch to RDS Multi-AZ with two readable standbys (cluster) which have faster failover than legacy Multi-AZ; combine with RDS Proxy.
C. Use a self-managed pgbouncer fronting RDS.
D. Disable the standby and rely on snapshots.
explanation: Multi-AZ DB cluster (with two readable standbys) typically fails over in under 35 seconds and supports RDS Proxy, which together hide the failover from clients. (A) helps but legacy Multi-AZ alone can take 60-120s. (C) is unmanaged and shares the same network signal problem. (D) reduces availability.

question: A product runs DynamoDB Global Tables across three Regions. Engineers want a runbook that, on a Region outage, drains write traffic to one specific Region and prevents conflicting writes during the outage window. Which mechanism is most appropriate?
options: A. Use Route 53 ARC routing controls plus an application-level write-region selector backed by Parameter Store; reads can continue from local replicas, and writes are pinned to a survivor Region. Conflict prevention is at the application layer because Global Tables uses last-writer-wins.
B. Use DynamoDB transactional writes to enforce conflicts.
C. Disable Global Tables temporarily.
D. Use TTL to age out conflicting records.
explanation: Global Tables resolves conflicts via last-writer-wins, so app-layer pinning is required. Routing controls give a clean failover signal for the data plane. (B) transactions are single-Region scope. (C) cannot disable Global Tables on the fly without data loss. (D) does not prevent conflict.

question: A team operating CloudFront with a custom origin needs to maintain availability when the primary origin returns 5xx. Which configuration is appropriate?
options: A. CloudFront origin group with primary and secondary origins, Origin Failover Criteria including 500/502/503/504, and origin shield enabled in a Region close to most viewers; combine with custom error pages for last-resort UX.
B. Increase CloudFront cache TTL to 24 hours and ignore origin health.
C. Use Lambda@Edge to retry the same origin five times.
D. Disable origin shield to reduce coupling.
explanation: Origin failover with the right error codes plus origin shield improves cache efficiency and resilience. (B) trades freshness for availability and breaks dynamic content. (C) compounds load on the failing origin. (D) origin shield is generally beneficial; disabling it does not improve resilience.

question: An ElastiCache Redis cluster sits behind a backend service. The team needs an automated failover that keeps the same connection string and triggers application alarms on lag spikes greater than 10 seconds. Which configuration provides this?
options: A. Enable Multi-AZ with automatic failover on the replication group, use the primary endpoint (which is updated on failover), and create CloudWatch alarms on the EngineCPUUtilization and ReplicationLag metrics.
B. Use a Network Load Balancer in front of Redis.
C. Use Redis Cluster mode disabled with manual failover only.
D. Place a Route 53 weighted record in front of each node.
explanation: The Redis replication group primary endpoint automatically points to the new primary on failover. ReplicationLag (or DatabaseMemoryUsagePercentage paired) is the metric for lag. (B) NLB does not understand Redis replication. (C) defeats automation. (D) Route 53 cannot reliably track replica state.

question: A company runs an active/passive multi-Region architecture. They need to verify quarterly that the passive Region can serve full production load. Which testing approach is best supported by AWS services?
options: A. Use AWS Resilience Hub assessments, run AWS FIS experiments simulating Region-impairing fault scenarios in pre-prod, and shift live traffic to passive via Route 53 ARC during a scheduled game day with ARC safety rules ensuring a clean state.
B. Email the team and ask them to do their best.
C. Use CloudWatch Synthetics canaries only.
D. Use DR backups to snapshot and restore each quarter.
explanation: Resilience Hub identifies gaps; FIS rehearses faults; ARC safely switches live traffic with auditable controls and safety rules. Synthetics alone do not load-test. Backup/restore drills test data, not capacity. (B) is not testing.

question: A failover-critical workload uses NLB with cross-zone load balancing. After a recent test, removing all instances from one AZ caused 2% of TCP connections to time out for ~30s before NLB stopped sending to that AZ. Which improvement reduces this?
options: A. Tune the NLB target group health check (interval, threshold) so unhealthy targets are removed faster, and ensure connection draining (deregistration delay) is set appropriately; combine with Route 53 health checks on a shorter interval for DNS-level shedding.
B. Disable cross-zone load balancing entirely to localize impact.
C. Switch to ALB which has faster failover.
D. Run only in a single AZ.
explanation: Reducing the health check interval and threshold removes unhealthy targets quicker; connection draining gives in-flight requests time to complete. (B) disabling cross-zone localizes impact but increases imbalance. (C) ALB is HTTP-only; if NLB is required this is not a swap. (D) reduces availability.

question: A multi-tenant SaaS uses Lambda extensively. After a regional event, the team wants a consolidated view of failed asynchronous Lambda invocations across the org, with the original payload retained. Which design captures this with minimal custom code?
options: A. Configure Lambda destinations on failure to an EventBridge bus; route to an SQS DLQ that holds payloads, plus an Athena partition projection over S3 logs; for org-wide visibility, deliver to a central security account via cross-account EventBridge bus.
B. Aggregate via custom DynamoDB tables per function.
C. Use CloudTrail data events on Lambda.
D. Print to logs and grep manually.
explanation: Lambda destinations on failure capture the input record alongside the error response. EventBridge cross-account buses make org-wide aggregation simple. Athena over S3 enables ad-hoc analysis. (B) is heavy custom code. (C) data events do not capture invocation payloads. (D) is not scalable.

question: A platform team must ingest CloudTrail, VPC Flow, and DNS query logs into a central security data lake to power 13-month retention searches and ad-hoc queries. Which design uses managed services and minimal ETL?
options: A. Use Security Lake to aggregate CloudTrail (management and data events), VPC Flow, and Route 53 Resolver query logs from member accounts via Organizations; query via Athena/QuickSight; lifecycle to Glacier for retention beyond 13 months.
B. Forward all logs to a self-hosted ELK cluster.
C. Use S3 with custom Lambda parsers per log type.
D. Use OpenSearch Service for everything with no S3 backing.
explanation: Security Lake natively normalizes the listed log sources to OCSF in Apache Parquet, integrates with Organizations, and allows Athena queries. Lifecycle to Glacier handles long-term retention. (B) is operational overhead. (C) reinvents Security Lake. (D) is expensive without S3 archival.

question: A team must alert when Lambda P99 duration is anomalously high but tolerate normal periodic batch peaks. Which CloudWatch primitive fits best?
options: A. A static threshold alarm at 1000ms.

B. CloudWatch Anomaly Detection on the Duration metric (statistic p99) with a band, combined with a composite alarm requiring breach AND a non-zero invocation rate; suppress during a known maintenance schedule with composite alarm action suppressor.
C. CloudWatch Metric Math step function.
D. CloudWatch Logs metric filter on the request id.
explanation: Anomaly detection learns the seasonal pattern of P99 duration; composite alarms with action suppressors avoid alerting on known maintenance. (A) cannot adapt to seasonality. (C) Metric Math is helpful but not on its own anomaly detection. (D) does not measure duration.

question: A company collects application logs from 1,500 EC2 instances. They want structured search, alerting, and the ability to write metric filters that emit to CloudWatch. Which agent and pattern are correct in 2026 best practice?
options: A. The unified CloudWatch agent with a JSON config that ships logs to CloudWatch Logs log groups, plus subscription filters that fan out to Kinesis Data Firehose to S3 for cold storage; metric filters on the log groups emit numeric metrics for alarms.
B. The legacy awslogs agent only, with no metric filters.
C. SSM Session Manager log shipping.
D. Custom rsyslog over UDP to CloudWatch.
explanation: The unified agent supersedes the legacy awslogs agent and supports both metrics and logs. Subscription filters tee logs to Firehose for archival/Athena. Metric filters give alarmable metrics. (B) is deprecated. (C) Session Manager logs interactive shells, not application logs. (D) is unmanaged.

question: A team operating a microservices fleet on EKS wants distributed tracing with low overhead, automatic context propagation through SQS and HTTP, and a clear service map. Which combination is appropriate?
options: A. AWS X-Ray with the OpenTelemetry-based ADOT (AWS Distro for OpenTelemetry) collector running as a DaemonSet, instrumented SDKs that propagate W3C traceparent and AWS X-Amzn-Trace-Id, sampling rules tuned per service.
B. CloudWatch Logs Insights queries.
C. VPC Flow Logs.
D. Custom request IDs in headers without collector.
explanation: ADOT collects OpenTelemetry traces and exports to X-Ray; sampling rules limit overhead; traceparent and X-Amzn-Trace-Id propagate through HTTP and SQS. (B) is logs, not traces. (C) flow logs are network-level. (D) lacks the service map.

question: An ECS Fargate workload is failing intermittently. The team wants per-container metrics (CPU, memory, network), application logs co-located, and to slice by container and task. Which solution provides this with minimal code changes?
options: A. CloudWatch Container Insights with enhanced observability for ECS, awslogs driver (or FireLens) for application logs, and Container Insights dashboards filtered by ClusterName, TaskDefinitionFamily, and ContainerName.
B. EC2 detailed monitoring.
C. CloudWatch Synthetics canaries only.
D. AWS App Mesh metrics only.
explanation: Container Insights surfaces task and container-level metrics; enhanced observability adds richer dimensions. FireLens lets you route logs to additional destinations if needed. (B) is host-level and misses container slicing. (C) probes externally. (D) is mesh-level only.

question: An app team's CloudTrail Lake event data store is too large to scan during incident hunts. They need fast targeted queries on management events for the last 30 days while retaining 7 years cheaply. Which configuration helps?
options: A. Tier the event data store with a hot retention of 30 days and extended retention pricing for the rest, partition queries by eventTime, and use CloudTrail Lake's federated queries from Athena where appropriate.
B. Replace Lake with a single S3 bucket and grep through CloudTrail JSON.
C. Use CloudWatch Logs with a 7-year retention.
D. Use AWS Config snapshot history.
explanation: CloudTrail Lake supports tiered retention (hot vs extended) and partition-aware queries; federated queries from Athena are available. (B) is unmanageable at scale. (C) Logs at 7 years is expensive and not optimized for trail queries. (D) Config has different scope.

question: A team needs an alarm that fires only when an EC2 instance has high CPU AND the same instance has high disk queue depth, ignoring transient single-metric spikes. Which CloudWatch construct is correct?
options: A. Two metric alarms combined with a composite alarm using ALARM(cpu) AND ALARM(diskQueue), with M-of-N evaluation periods on each child alarm.
B. A single alarm with two thresholds (not supported).
C. A CloudWatch dashboard that operators watch.
D. A Lambda that queries CloudWatch every minute.
explanation: Composite alarms combine child alarms via boolean logic and respect each child's evaluation periods, removing transient spikes. (B) is not a feature. (C) is not an alarm. (D) reinvents alarms with worse reliability.

question: A team requires that all CloudWatch Logs across the org are encrypted with a CMK and can never be deleted within their retention window. Which combination provides this?
options: A. Apply an SCP that denies logs:DisassociateKmsKey and logs:DeleteRetentionPolicy and logs:DeleteLogGroup; require associated KMS keys via Config rule cloudwatch-log-group-encrypted; for true immutability also export to an S3 bucket with Object Lock compliance mode.
B. Just enable KMS by hand.
C. Use SCP to deny logs:CreateLogGroup.
D. CloudWatch Logs has built-in WORM.
explanation: CloudWatch Logs do not have native WORM; immutability requires export to S3 Object Lock. SCPs prevent retention and key changes. The Config rule enforces encryption. (B) is not enforcement. (C) prevents log creation entirely. (D) is false.

question: A team wants a daily report on AWS spend anomalies and has thousands of resources across 30 accounts. Which managed approach is appropriate?
options: A. AWS Cost Anomaly Detection with monitors per service and per account, alerts via SNS to a Slack ChatBot channel; combine with Cost and Usage Reports in Athena for deep dive.
B. CloudWatch billing alarm only.
C. Manual review of Cost Explorer.
D. CloudTrail billing data events.
explanation: Cost Anomaly Detection is purpose-built for anomaly alerts and integrates with SNS and ChatBot. CUR plus Athena enables deep analysis. (B) only triggers on absolute thresholds. (C) is not automated. (D) is not a feature.

question: A team manages 80 Lambda functions and wants automatic baseline alarms for Errors, Throttles, and Duration without writing CloudFormation per function. Which approach is appropriate?
options: A. CloudWatch Application Insights with SAM/CloudFormation-defined Lambda resources, plus a generic CDK Aspect that adds standard alarms to every Lambda construct in the app.
B. Edit each function in the console.
C. Use only CloudWatch Synthetics.
D. Use AWS Config rules to evaluate alarm coverage instead of creating alarms.
explanation: Application Insights auto-detects Lambda issues with curated alarms; a CDK Aspect can systematically add baseline alarms to every Lambda construct. (B) does not scale. (C) is external probing. (D) measures coverage, not signal.

question: A finance app uses AWS Glue jobs orchestrated by Step Functions. Operators need to know when a job has been running 25% longer than its rolling P95 baseline. Which approach is best?
options: A. Use CloudWatch Metrics Insights to compute P95 over a rolling window, push the comparison via Metric Math against the current run duration, and alarm when current exceeds P95 by 25%; tag the alarm to suppress during scheduled large-load periods using composite alarm action suppressors.
B. Static alarm at 1 hour.
C. Use AWS Config to flag long-running jobs.
D. Use SES email to operators.
explanation: Metrics Insights and Metric Math allow rolling baselines and percentile math without custom code; action suppressors handle planned outages. Static alarms (B) cannot adapt. Config does not measure runtime. SES is delivery, not detection.

question: A fintech needs alerting via Slack for high-severity GuardDuty findings, with an ack workflow and a 15-minute SLO from finding to engineer ack. Which combination is correct?
options: A. GuardDuty findings to EventBridge filtered by severity >= 7.0, fanned out to AWS Chatbot in a dedicated Slack channel, plus a Lambda that records ack time when an engineer reacts; surface SLO via CloudWatch metric and alarm.
B. SNS email only.
C. Log to CloudWatch and rely on dashboards.
D. PagerDuty only without an EventBridge filter.
explanation: EventBridge filters by severity, ChatBot delivers to Slack with managed integrations, and the ack metric closes the loop. (B) email cannot capture ack reliably. (C) dashboards are passive. (D) PagerDuty is fine, but a filter is mandatory or noise overwhelms responders.

question: A team experienced an incident where an IAM access key was exposed in a public GitHub repo. They need automated remediation: disable the key, rotate it, and isolate the EC2 instances using it. Which design is correct?
options: A. AWS Health and AWS_RISK_CREDENTIALS_EXPOSED events route via EventBridge to an SSM Automation runbook that disables and recreates the access key, opens a Security Hub finding, and triggers a Lambda that quarantines instances by replacing their security group with a deny-all SG.
B. Manually delete the key when an engineer notices.
C. Enable MFA on the IAM user and ignore the leak.
D. Rotate keys daily and assume leaks are inevitable.
explanation: AWS publishes AWS_RISK_CREDENTIALS_EXPOSED Health events when it detects keys in public sources. EventBridge to Automation provides reliable remediation. SG swap is a fast quarantine. (B) is too slow. (C) does not contain the leak. (D) is not enough.

question: An on-call engineer needs to safely run privileged commands on a production EC2 instance during a Sev-1 with full audit and a justification. Which workflow is appropriate?
options: A. Session Manager with an IAM policy condition requiring an aws:RequestTag/JustificationId on StartSession, session logs to KMS-encrypted S3, and an EventBridge rule that posts the justification and operator to Slack and Security Hub for review.
B. SSH from a bastion using a shared key.
C. Use root credentials and rotate after the incident.
D. Disable IAM and set instance role to admin temporarily.
explanation: Session Manager with policy conditions enforces justification and audits sessions. (B) and (C) are insecure and untraceable. (D) creates a privilege escalation gap.

question: A SaaS team must respond to AWS Config rule violations within five minutes. The rule s3-bucket-public-read-prohibited fires for a misconfigured bucket. Which automatic remediation works without writing custom Lambdas?
options: A. Configure AWS Config remediation with the SSM Automation document AWS-DisableS3BucketPublicReadWrite as an automatic remediation action with auto-remediation enabled and retry parameters set; concurrent execution and EventBridge notification provide visibility.
B. Use AWS Lambda triggered by S3 events.
C. Use IAM policies on every user.
D. Use Trusted Advisor only.
explanation: Config supports automatic remediation via SSM Automation; AWS-DisableS3BucketPublicReadWrite is the canned doc. (B) S3 events do not fire on policy changes reliably. (C) does not auto-fix existing buckets. (D) Trusted Advisor reports but does not auto-remediate.

question: A Sev-2 alert says ALB 5xx is elevated but only for one target group. The team wants a runbook that automatically (1) captures a snapshot of CloudWatch metrics and X-Ray traces, (2) gathers logs from the affected tasks, and (3) opens a ticket. Which orchestration is appropriate?
options: A. EventBridge rule on the alarm action -> Step Functions express workflow that calls SDKs for CloudWatch GetMetricData and X-Ray GetTraceSummaries, gathers logs via Logs Insights queries, posts a comment to ServiceNow, and stores a bundle in S3 referenced from the ticket.
B. PagerDuty integration only.
C. SNS to email only.
D. Manual runbook in a wiki.
explanation: Step Functions orchestrates the data-gathering steps reliably. EventBridge triggers it from the alarm. (B) only pages humans. (C) is delivery, not orchestration. (D) defeats automation.

question: An incident requires forensic preservation of an EC2 instance: the team must isolate it from the network, capture memory and disk, and retain artifacts immutably. Which sequence is correct?
options: A. Detach the instance from its Auto Scaling group (StandBy lifecycle), replace the security group with a forensic deny-all, snapshot EBS volumes with copy-tags, capture memory via SSM Run Command running an approved tool, copy artifacts to an S3 bucket with Object Lock compliance mode, and tag the case ID; document via Security Hub finding.
B. Stop the instance immediately to prevent harm.
C. Terminate the instance and rely on logs.
D. Reboot the instance and grab the post-reboot disk.
explanation: Standby preserves the instance; deny-all SG isolates; snapshots preserve disk state; SSM captures memory; Object Lock provides chain of custody. Stopping (B) loses memory. Termination (C) destroys evidence. Rebooting (D) corrupts evidence.

question: A platform must detect when an unintended IAM role gains AdministratorAccess and revoke within minutes. Which detection is most reliable?
options: A. EventBridge rule on AttachRolePolicy or PutRolePolicy with policy ARN arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AdministratorAccess, plus IAM Access Analyzer external access findings; on match, an SSM Automation runbook detaches the policy and notifies via Security Hub.
B. CloudTrail Insights only.
C. AWS Config rule once a day.
D. Trusted Advisor periodic reports.
explanation: EventBridge can match the specific API and policy ARN in near real time, while Access Analyzer covers indirect paths. (B) Insights detects unusual call volume but is broader. (C) Config once-a-day is too slow. (D) Trusted Advisor is periodic and limited.

question: A team needs a single pane of glass that aggregates GuardDuty, Inspector v2, Macie, and IAM Access Analyzer findings across the org with consistent severity normalization and the ability to send to ServiceNow. Which service is the right hub?
options: A. AWS Security Hub with Organization-wide enablement, security standards enabled per the team's framework, EventBridge filtering by severity to a ServiceNow integration; configure cross-region aggregation and finding aggregation in the delegated administrator account.
B. CloudWatch Logs.
C. Macie alone.
D. CloudTrail Lake.
explanation: Security Hub centralizes the named services, normalizes to ASFF, and supports Organizations-level aggregation. (B), (C), (D) each cover only one slice.

question: A storefront service throttles when DynamoDB latency spikes. The team wants the on-call channel to receive a 'panic' page only if DDB latency AND retries AND application 5xx rise together. Which CloudWatch construct fits?
options: A. A composite alarm combining three metric alarms with AND logic, using action suppressors to silence during scheduled bulk imports, and routing to PagerDuty via SNS.
B. Three independent alarms paged separately.
C. A single anomaly detection alarm on the LatencyAvg metric.
D. Set CloudWatch alarms to send only to email.
explanation: Composite alarms model correlated incidents, suppressors handle planned events, and PagerDuty via SNS provides escalation. Splitting alarms (B) creates noise. Anomaly on a single metric (C) is too narrow. Email (D) does not page.

question: A team wants to auto-create incident records and timeline events whenever a critical CloudWatch alarm fires, including links to the runbook and the ability to open a war room. Which AWS service is purpose-built for this?
options: A. AWS Systems Manager Incident Manager with response plans linking the alarm to runbooks and chat channels (Chatbot), automation documents for triage, and post-incident analysis templates.
B. Trusted Advisor.
C. Personal Health Dashboard.
D. CloudFormation StackSets.
explanation: Incident Manager is the AWS-native incident response service: response plans, runbooks, engagement plans, and chat channels. The other answers do not perform incident orchestration.

question: A team noticed anomalous EC2 launches in a development account by a long-dormant access key. They want to prevent any future unfamiliar-region API call by that account globally and automatically. Which control fits?
options: A. Apply an SCP that denies actions outside an allowed Region list (aws:RequestedRegion), and an IAM permission boundary on the dev account roles; pair with GuardDuty UnusualBehavior findings and an EventBridge rule that auto-disables the offending key on detection.
B. Remove all IAM users from the account.
C. Use CloudFront geo-blocking.
D. Use VPC NACLs to restrict egress to specific regions.
explanation: Region restriction is enforced via aws:RequestedRegion in SCP/IAM, not network-layer ACLs. GuardDuty Unusual* findings detect the behavior and an EventBridge rule + SSM disables the key fast. (B) is overkill. (C) is for HTTP. (D) NACLs do not understand AWS Regions.

question: A startup runs a stateful workload on EC2 with EBS gp3. They want to recover from accidental deletion within 15 minutes, across two Regions, with consolidated backup policy management. Which service combination is correct?
options: A. AWS Backup with cross-Region copy enabled in the backup plan, vault lock in compliance mode for retention enforcement, and EventBridge alerts on backup job failures; restore via Backup restore jobs.
B. Manual EBS snapshots and a cron job.
C. DLM only, no cross-Region.
D. AWS Elastic Disaster Recovery (DRS) configured to replicate to S3 only.
explanation: AWS Backup centralizes EBS backup policy, supports cross-Region copy, and Vault Lock prevents tampering (compliance mode is irreversible). Restore jobs deliver the volume back. DLM (C) is single-Region. DRS (D) replicates to a staging area but the option distorts how DRS works (replicates to a staging subnet in the recovery Region, not S3 only). (B) is fragile.

question: A government workload requires testing actual fault conditions during business hours, with safety controls ensuring blast radius stays within a single account and stop conditions on production alarms. Which service is appropriate?
options: A. AWS Fault Injection Service (FIS) experiments using resource tags for targeting, stop conditions tied to CloudWatch alarms, and IAM execution role limited to the experiment account.
B. AWS Config remediation actions.
C. CloudFormation Drift simulation.
D. CodeDeploy bake time only.
explanation: FIS is the managed chaos service: it targets resources by tag/filter, supports stop conditions via CloudWatch alarms, and is account-scoped via its execution role. (B) repairs misconfig, not fault injection. (C) is not a real feature. (D) is unrelated.

question: A platform team must enforce that IAM roles created in any member account never grant a role-to-role trust to an account outside the Organization. Which mechanism is correct?
options: A. AWS IAM Access Analyzer at the organization scope to detect external access (zone of trust = the org), plus an SCP using aws:PrincipalOrgID condition keys on iam:CreateRole and iam:UpdateAssumeRolePolicy that denies trust policies whose principal is outside the org.
B. Trusted Advisor IAM checks.
C. CloudTrail Insights.
D. Block all sts:AssumeRole at the network level.
explanation: Access Analyzer at org scope finds existing external access. SCPs with aws:PrincipalOrgID prevent creating trust policies with outside principals. (B) is informational. (C) detects anomalies, not policy. (D) sts:AssumeRole is API, not network.

question: A regulator requires that database credentials in a production RDS workload be rotated every 30 days with no downtime, and that no human can read the credentials. Which approach is appropriate?
options: A. AWS Secrets Manager with managed rotation for RDS using the multi-user rotation strategy (alternating two database users), KMS encryption with a customer-managed key, IAM policies that grant only the application role secretsmanager:GetSecretValue and deny GetSecretValue to humans via SCP.
B. Store credentials in SSM Parameter Store SecureString and rotate via a cron Lambda.
C. Hash credentials in a config file.
D. Use IAM database authentication only.
explanation: Secrets Manager managed rotation handles RDS rotation; the multi-user strategy avoids downtime. SCPs prevent humans from reading the secret. (B) lacks managed RDS rotation hooks and risks downtime. (C) is plain insecure. (D) IAM auth is good but not all engines/clients support it for every use case; the question specifies credentials.

question: A workload uses KMS keys across many services. The security team requires automatic annual rotation, and a way to test that key rotation does not break encryption. Which configuration is correct?
options: A. Use AWS-managed CMKs where possible (auto-rotate), enable rotation on customer-managed CMKs (annual by default with a configurable rotation period), retain prior key material for decryption of older ciphertexts, and run a test that re-encrypts a sample of objects using the new key version while validating downstream services.
B. Generate keys outside AWS and import without rotation.
C. Disable rotation to avoid downtime.
D. Use SSE-S3 only and forget KMS.
explanation: AWS KMS retains old key material so existing ciphertexts continue to decrypt while new encryption uses the new version. AWS-managed keys auto-rotate; customer-managed keys can rotate on a configurable cadence. (B) external imports cannot be auto-rotated. (C) misses the requirement. (D) is not key management.

question: A team operates an internal PKI for service-to-service mTLS. They want a managed CA hierarchy with audit logs, scheduled CRL generation to S3, and short-lived certificates rotated by ACM. Which AWS combination fits?
options: A. AWS Private CA (formerly ACM Private CA) with a root and subordinate CA hierarchy, CRL configured to S3 with a CloudFront distribution if needed for HTTPS access, and ACM-issued private certificates auto-rotated with permission delegation.
B. Self-signed certificates in Secrets Manager.
C. ACM public certificates only.
D. AWS IoT Device Defender certificates.
explanation: Private CA gives the managed CA hierarchy with audit. CRLs publish to S3 (and optionally CloudFront for public CRL delivery). ACM Private cert issuance handles auto-rotation when integrated with services. (B) is unmanaged. (C) public certs cannot sign internal services. (D) is for IoT only.

question: A government tenant must prove compliance with a control set and produce evidence packages monthly. Which AWS service automates evidence collection?
options: A. AWS Audit Manager with a framework (e.g., FedRAMP, NIST 800-53), automatic evidence collection from CloudTrail, Config, Security Hub, and License Manager, exportable evidence packages, and delegated administrator across the org.
B. AWS Artifact alone.
C. AWS Config alone.
D. CloudTrail Lake alone.
explanation: Audit Manager is the managed evidence-collection service that produces compliance packages. Artifact provides AWS attestations to download but not evidence about your workload. Config and CloudTrail Lake supply data but do not assemble framework-aligned evidence packs.

question: A SaaS team wants to ensure that S3 buckets across the org never become public, and that any attempt to disable account-level S3 Block Public Access is denied. Which control is most direct?
options: A. SCP that denies s3:PutAccountPublicAccessBlock when removing block public access settings, plus s3:PutBucketPublicAccessBlock denying disabling on buckets, plus AWS Config managed rule s3-account-level-public-access-blocks-periodic for detection; remediate via SSM Automation.
B. AWS WAF in front of S3.
C. Use bucket ACLs.
D. Rely on Macie classifications.
explanation: Block Public Access is the canonical control; SCPs prevent its disablement, Config detects drift, and Automation remediates. (B) WAF is for HTTP, not S3 access. (C) ACLs are legacy and weak. (D) Macie classifies but does not control access.

question: A team must scan EC2, Lambda, and ECR images continuously for vulnerabilities and produce a single deduplicated finding view per resource. Which service is appropriate?
options: A. Amazon Inspector v2 enabled organization-wide for EC2, Lambda, and ECR with delegated admin in the security account; findings flow to Security Hub for dashboards.
B. GuardDuty.
C. Trusted Advisor.
D. Amazon Detective.
explanation: Inspector v2 covers EC2, Lambda, and ECR vulnerability scanning. (B) GuardDuty is threat detection, not vulnerability assessment. (C) Trusted Advisor is best-practice checks. (D) Detective focuses on investigation, not vuln scanning.

question: A healthcare team must restrict AWS API access to corporate networks AND block use from countries on a sanctions list. Which control fits each?
options: A. SCPs and IAM policies with aws:SourceIp / aws:VpcSourceIp conditions for corporate networks, and aws:SourceIp combined with an automated allow-list maintained from the sanctions list; for HTTP-layer blocking, AWS WAF geo-match on CloudFront or ALB. AWS does not natively offer geo-aware IAM conditions, so IP-based allowlisting is necessary.
B. IAM aws:RequestedRegion only.
C. Security groups per country.
D. KMS Key Policies with country conditions.
explanation: IAM does not have a country condition; aws:SourceIp with an allowlist that excludes sanctioned ranges is the IAM-level option. WAF provides HTTP-layer geo-match on CloudFront or ALB. (B) restricts Region of API call, not the requester country. (C) and (D) cannot do country-based logic.

question: A team needs to detect resource creation that violates internal naming or tagging conventions and prevent the creation when possible. Which combination is right?
options: A. AWS Organizations Tag Policies for inherited tag rules; AWS Config managed rule required-tags for detection; SCPs using aws:RequestTag and aws:TagKeys conditions to deny creation without required tags; remediation via SSM Automation if needed.
B. CloudFormation hooks alone.
C. CloudTrail metric filters.
D. Service Control Policies on resource names only.
explanation: Tag Policies enforce tag standards across the org; SCP conditions like aws:RequestTag block creates that lack required tags; Config detects after the fact. (B) hooks help only for CFN-driven creates. (C) is detective only. (D) cannot enforce arbitrary tag content.

question: A regulated team must redact PII from CloudWatch Logs before they leave the account. Which design fits?
options: A. CloudWatch Logs data protection policies with masked patterns for the relevant managed and custom data identifiers, plus subscription filters that ship to Firehose with a Lambda transformer for additional custom redactions; KMS encryption and access policies prevent log readers from unmasking by default.
B. Macie scanning only.
C. Manual log redaction by SREs.
D. Only encrypt at rest with KMS and assume that is enough.
explanation: Data protection policies are native to CloudWatch Logs and mask data identifiers in real time. Subscription filters plus a Lambda transformer extend coverage. Macie (B) targets S3 and does not redact in CWL. (C) is unscalable. (D) encryption does not redact.

question: A team must demonstrate that all production VPCs reject any inbound internet traffic except through a centralized inspection VPC. Which architecture and verification approach is correct?
options: A. AWS Network Firewall in a centralized inspection VPC fronted by a Transit Gateway (or AWS Cloud WAN core network) with appliance-mode attachments; Reachability Analyzer and Network Access Analyzer continuously verify that no production VPC has a path that bypasses the inspection VPC; AWS Firewall Manager provides org-wide policy enforcement.
B. Per-VPC NAT gateways with NACL rules.
C. Internet gateway with security groups.
D. CloudFront in front of every workload.
explanation: Centralized inspection via Network Firewall and TGW (or Cloud WAN) with appliance mode is the canonical pattern. Network Access Analyzer enforces no-bypass invariants and Reachability Analyzer verifies specific paths; Firewall Manager applies the policy across the org. The other options do not provide centralized, verifiable inspection.
